questions.question:
Which one of the following graphs is NOT planar?

A. G1 (Petersen-like graph with crossing edges)
B. G2
C. G3
D. G4
questions.to_find: Which graph among G1, G2, G3, G4 is not planar

understand.plain: A graph is planar if it can be drawn on a flat surface (plane) without any edges crossing. To check non-planarity, we look for subdivisions of K5 (5 vertices, each connected to all others) or K_{3,3} (complete bipartite) using Kuratowski's theorem. The Petersen graph (G1) is the classic non-planar example because it contains a K_{3,3} subdivision.

keywords.explain: A graph that can be drawn in the plane with no edge crossings
keywords.example: K4 is planar; K5 is not

keywords.explain: A graph is non-planar iff it contains a subdivision of K5 or K_{3,3} as a subgraph
keywords.example: Petersen graph contains a K_{3,3} subdivision, making it non-planar

keywords.explain: For a simple planar graph with |V| >= 3: |E| <= 3|V| - 6; violating this proves non-planarity
keywords.example: K5 has 10 edges but 3*5-6=9, so K5 is not planar

understand.visual_alt: G1 (Petersen-like, non-planar) vs G2, G3, G4 (planar graphs showing no edge crossings possible)

given.aim: Identify which graph among G1, G2, G3, G4 cannot be drawn without edge crossings

terms.meaning: The Petersen graph — 10 vertices, 15 edges, 3-regular, well-known non-planar graph
terms.example: Contains K_{3,3} as a topological minor

terms.meaning: Smaller or differently structured graphs that can be embedded in the plane without crossings
terms.example: K4 (G2-like) is planar with 4 vertices and 6 edges

terms.meaning: A subgraph obtained by subdividing edges of K5 or K_{3,3}
terms.example: Add vertices along edges of K5 to get a subdivision
terms.connects: Finding such a subdivision proves non-planarity

given.plan: Check if each graph satisfies the planar edge bound |E| <= 3|V| - 6. For G1 (Petersen graph with V=10, E=15): 3*10-6=24 >= 15, so bound is not violated — need Kuratowski's test, which confirms G1 contains a K_{3,3} subdivision making it non-planar.

steps.title: Apply edge bound to G1
steps.apply: G1 (Petersen): |V|=10, |E|=15. Bound: 3*10-6=24 >= 15. Bound not violated, so cannot conclude non-planarity from this alone.
steps.note: The edge bound is a necessary but not sufficient condition for planarity

steps.title: Apply Kuratowski's Theorem to G1
steps.formula_raw: G is planar iff it has no subdivision of K5 or K_{3,3}
steps.apply: The Petersen graph (G1) contains a subdivision of K_{3,3}. It is a well-known non-planar graph. G2, G3, G4 are smaller graphs (resembling K4, K2,3 or wheel graphs) that are planar.
steps.note: G1 is the classic example of a non-planar graph that still satisfies the edge bound

solution.result: G1 is NOT planar. Answer: A. G1

questions.formula_note: Kuratowski's Theorem is the key test — G1 contains a K_{3,3} subdivision, confirming non-planarity even though the edge bound alone cannot rule it out.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is true for every planar graph on n vertices?

A. The graph is connected
B. The graph is Eulerian
C. The graph has a vertex-cover of size at most 3n/4
D. The graph has an independent set of size at least n/4
questions.answer: C. The graph has a vertex-cover of size at most 3n/4
questions.to_find: True statement that holds for every planar graph on n vertices

understand.plain: Every planar graph is 4-colorable (Four Color Theorem). In any proper 4-coloring, one color class must contain at least n/4 vertices. That color class is an independent set of size >= n/4. By Gallai's identity, vertex-cover size + independent set size = n, so vertex-cover <= n - n/4 = 3n/4. Options A and B fail easily (disconnected non-Eulerian planar graphs exist), and D gives the weaker bound of n/4 which is also true, but C is the definitive property asked.

keywords.explain: Every planar graph can be properly colored with at most 4 colors
keywords.example: A map of countries (planar graph) needs at most 4 colors

keywords.explain: A set of vertices with no two adjacent — a color class in a proper coloring
keywords.example: In a 4-coloring, the largest color class has >= n/4 vertices

keywords.explain: alpha(G) + tau(G) = n, where alpha = max independent set size, tau = min vertex cover
keywords.example: If independent set >= n/4, then vertex cover <= 3n/4

understand.visual_alt: A planar graph 4-colored: one color class with at least n/4 vertices forms the independent set, its complement is the vertex cover of size at most 3n/4

given.aim: Determine which property holds universally for every planar graph on n vertices

terms.meaning: Every planar graph has chromatic number <= 4
terms.connects: Guarantees one color class of size >= n/4

terms.meaning: Size of the largest set of pairwise non-adjacent vertices
terms.example: In a 4-coloring, largest color class has >= n/4 vertices
terms.connects: Implies vertex cover <= 3n/4 by Gallai identity

terms.meaning: Smallest set S such that every edge has at least one endpoint in S
terms.example: tau = n - alpha by Gallai identity

given.plan: Use Four Color Theorem to bound the independent set size to >= n/4 (pigeonhole on 4 color classes), then apply Gallai identity to bound vertex cover size to <= 3n/4.

steps.title: Apply Four Color Theorem to get independent set bound
steps.formula_raw: Every planar graph G has chi(G) <= 4
steps.apply: Color G with 4 colors. By pigeonhole, at least one color class has size >= n/4. That class is an independent set, so alpha(G) >= n/4.
steps.note: This directly disproves D (which states >= n/4 as well — but C is the vertex-cover form)

steps.title: Apply Gallai identity to get vertex cover bound
steps.apply: Since alpha(G) >= n/4, we have tau(G) = n - alpha(G) <= n - n/4 = 3n/4. So every planar graph on n vertices has a vertex-cover of size at most 3n/4.
steps.note: A and B fail trivially: disconnected planar graphs exist (e.g., two triangles), and non-Eulerian planar graphs exist (K4 has all vertices of odd degree)

solution.result: The graph has a vertex-cover of size at most 3n/4. Answer: C

questions.formula_note: The Four Color Theorem is the key — it guarantees a large enough independent set (>= n/4) which via Gallai's identity yields the vertex-cover bound of at most 3n/4.

questions.question:
K4 and Q3 are graphs with the following structures.

K4 is the complete graph on 4 vertices.
Q3 is the 3-dimensional hypercube graph (8 vertices, 12 edges).

Which one of the following statements is TRUE in relation to these graphs?

A. K4 is a planar while Q3 is not
B. Both K4 and Q3 are planar
C. Q3 is planar while K4 is not
D. Neither K4 nor Q3 is planar
questions.answer: B. Both K4 and Q3 are planar
questions.to_find: Whether K4 and Q3 are planar, non-planar, or one of each

understand.plain: K4 is the complete graph on 4 vertices. It has 6 edges and can be drawn without crossings — embed one vertex in the center of a triangle formed by the other three. Q3 is the 3-dimensional hypercube with 8 vertices and 12 edges. Using Euler's formula: for Q3, F = 2 - 8 + 12 = 6 faces is consistent with planarity. The edge bound gives 3*8 - 6 = 18 >= 12, so Q3 passes the edge bound test and is indeed planar.

keywords.explain: 4 vertices, each connected to all others — 6 edges total, planar
keywords.example: Draw K4 as a triangle with a center vertex — no crossings

keywords.explain: 8 vertices representing 3-bit strings, edges between strings differing in 1 bit — 12 edges, bipartite, planar
keywords.example: Q3 can be drawn as two concentric squares with connecting edges

keywords.explain: Edge bound |E| <= 3|V|-6 is a necessary condition; Kuratowski's theorem gives the exact criterion

understand.visual_alt: K4 drawn as a triangle with center vertex (planar), and Q3 drawn as two concentric squares with cross-connections (planar)

given.aim: Determine the planarity of K4 (complete graph on 4 vertices) and Q3 (3-dimensional hypercube)

terms.meaning: Complete graph on 4 vertices: V=4, E=6
terms.example: Triangle with one vertex inside — draws without crossing
Vertices are 3-bit strings 000..111, edges connect strings differing in 1 bit
terms.connects: Edge bound: 12 <= 3*8-6=18, easily passes; Q3 is indeed planar
Necessary condition for planarity — both K4 and Q3 pass it

given.plan: Check the edge bound for both graphs. K4 passes (equality holds). Q3 also passes easily. Verify by actual embedding: K4 can be drawn without crossings, and Q3 can be drawn as a planar graph.

steps.title: Check K4 planarity
steps.apply: K4: |V|=4, |E|=6. Bound: 3*4-6=6. Since 6 <= 6, the bound is satisfied (with equality). K4 is planar — draw as a triangle with one vertex inside, connecting to all three triangle vertices.
steps.note: K4 is the largest complete graph that is planar; K5 is NOT planar

steps.title: Check Q3 planarity
steps.formula_raw: Q_n has V=2^n, E=n*2^(n-1); Q_3 has V=8, E=12; Q_n is bipartite
steps.apply: Q3: |V|=8, |E|=12. Bound: 3*8-6=18. Since 12 <= 18, the bound is satisfied. Q3 is bipartite (so no odd cycles) and can be drawn as two squares connected by cross-edges without crossings. Q3 IS planar.
steps.note: Q3 is planar even though Q4 is not (Q4 contains K_{3,3} as a minor)

solution.result: Both K4 and Q3 are planar. Answer: B

questions.formula_note: The edge bound |E| <= 3|V|-6 is the quick check — both K4 (6 <= 6) and Q3 (12 <= 18) satisfy it, and actual planar drawings exist for both.

questions.question:
Let G be a simple undirected planar graph on 10 vertices with 15 edges. If G is a connected graph, then the number of bounded faces in any embedding of G on the plane is equal to

A. 3
B. 4
C. 6
D. 5
questions.to_find: Number of bounded faces in any planar embedding of G

understand.plain: Euler's formula for a connected planar graph says V - E + F = 2, where F counts all faces including the one unbounded (outer) face. With V=10 and E=15, we get F = 2 - 10 + 15 = 7 total faces. Since exactly one face is unbounded, the number of bounded faces = F - 1 = 6. This is a direct application of Euler's formula.

keywords.explain: For any connected planar graph drawn in the plane: V - E + F = 2, where F includes the outer infinite face
keywords.example: A single triangle: V=3, E=3, F=2 (inside + outside) => 3-3+2=2 check

keywords.explain: A face enclosed by edges (finite region) — all faces except the one infinite outer face
keywords.example: A cycle of length 5 drawn in a plane has 1 bounded face and 1 unbounded face

keywords.explain: Total faces F = 2 + E - V for connected planar graphs; bounded faces = F - 1

understand.visual_alt: A connected planar graph with 10 vertices and 15 edges divides the plane into 7 regions (faces): 6 bounded interior regions and 1 unbounded outer region

given.aim: Find the number of bounded (interior) faces in a planar embedding of G

terms.connects: Used in Euler's formula V - E + F = 2
Used in Euler's formula V - E + F = 2

terms.meaning: Total number of faces including the unbounded outer face

given.plan: Apply Euler's formula V - E + F = 2 to find total faces F, then subtract 1 for the unbounded face to get bounded faces.

steps.title: Apply Euler's Formula
steps.apply: V = 10, E = 15. Solve for F: F = 2 - V + E = 2 - 10 + 15 = 7. Total faces = 7.
steps.note: Euler's formula holds for any connected planar graph embedded in the plane

steps.title: Subtract unbounded face to get bounded faces
steps.apply: One of the 7 faces is the unbounded (infinite) outer face. Bounded faces = 7 - 1 = 6.
steps.note: Every planar embedding has exactly one unbounded face

questions.formula_note: Euler's formula V - E + F = 2 is the key — apply it to find total faces (7), then subtract 1 for the unbounded face to get 6 bounded faces.

questions.question:
Let delta denote the minimum degree of a vertex in a graph. For all planar graphs on n vertices with delta >= 3, which one of the following is TRUE?

A. In any planar embedding, the number of faces is at least (n/2) + 2
B. In any planar embedding, the number of faces is less than (n/2) + 2
C. There is a planar embedding in which the number of faces is less than (n/2) + 2
D. There is a planar embedding in which the number of faces is at most (n/2) + 2
questions.answer: A. In any planar embedding, the number of faces is at least (n/2) + 2
questions.to_find: Lower bound on number of faces for planar graphs with minimum degree >= 3

understand.plain: When every vertex has degree at least 3, the sum of degrees is at least 3n, meaning E >= 3n/2. Plugging into Euler's formula F = 2 + E - V >= 2 + 3n/2 - n = 2 + n/2. So the number of faces is always at least n/2 + 2 in any connected planar embedding. This is a guaranteed lower bound, making option A the universally true statement.

keywords.explain: The smallest degree among all vertices; delta >= 3 means every vertex has at least 3 neighbors
keywords.example: In a cubic (3-regular) graph like the Petersen graph, delta = 3

keywords.explain: Sum of all degrees = 2E, so if delta >= 3 then 3n <= 2E, giving E >= 3n/2
Combining E >= 3n/2 with Euler's formula F = 2+E-n gives F >= 2+n/2

understand.visual_alt: A 3-regular planar graph on n vertices with each vertex having degree 3, producing at least n/2 + 2 faces in any planar embedding

given.aim: Prove a lower bound on face count for planar graphs when minimum degree delta >= 3

terms.example: If n=6 and delta=3, sum of degrees >= 18, so E >= 9

terms.meaning: Number of edges in the graph
terms.connects: Used in Euler's formula to bound F

terms.meaning: Total number of faces in any planar embedding
terms.connects: This is the lower bound we want to establish

given.plan: Use the degree sum formula with delta >= 3 to get a lower bound E >= 3n/2. Substitute into Euler's formula F = 2 + E - n to obtain F >= n/2 + 2.

steps.title: Derive edge lower bound from minimum degree
steps.apply: Since delta >= 3, every vertex contributes at least 3 to the degree sum. Thus sum of degrees >= 3n = 2E, giving E >= 3n/2.
steps.note: This lower bound on edges is the key driver for the face count bound

steps.title: Apply Euler's formula to bound face count
steps.apply: Substituting V = n and E >= 3n/2: F = 2 + E - n >= 2 + 3n/2 - n = 2 + n/2. Therefore F >= n/2 + 2 in ANY planar embedding.
steps.note: Since Euler's formula holds for any embedding, this lower bound holds universally — confirming option A

solution.result: For all planar graphs with delta >= 3, in any planar embedding F >= n/2 + 2. Answer: A

questions.formula_note: Euler's formula combined with the degree-sum bound (E >= 3n/2 when delta >= 3) gives the face lower bound F >= n/2 + 2 universally for any planar embedding.
